root.narration: Oh no! Your opponent's knight just jumped to a square where it can capture your queen OR your pawn next turn. You can only save one piece. Which piece do you move to safety?

choices.text: Move the queen to safety! The queen is the most powerful piece on the board!
Move the pawn to safety! Every piece matters!
Ignore the threat and move a different piece somewhere else.

outcome-great.narration: Great choice! You move your queen to a safe square. Your opponent's knight captures your pawn, but that's okay. A pawn is only worth 1 point, but your queen is worth 9 points! You saved the most valuable piece on the board!

outcome.headline: Smart Save! You Protected Your Queen!
outcome.explanation: In chess, every piece has a point value. The queen is worth 9 points -- she is the most powerful piece! A rook is worth 5, a bishop or knight is worth 3, and a pawn is only worth 1. When you have to choose which piece to save, always save the one worth more points. Losing a pawn is no big deal compared to losing your queen!
outcome.whatToRemember: Queen = 9, Rook = 5, Bishop = 3, Knight = 3, Pawn = 1. Always save the more valuable piece!

keyTerms.definition: How many points each chess piece is worth. The queen is worth the most (9 points) and the pawn is worth the least (1 point).
The total value of all your pieces. Having more material usually means you are winning!

outcome-okay.narration: You move a different piece and ignore the threat. Your opponent's knight captures your queen! That is a huge loss. Your queen was worth 9 points and now she is gone.

outcome.headline: Oops! You Lost Your Queen!
outcome.explanation: When a piece is being attacked, you need to pay attention! The queen is the most powerful piece on the board, worth 9 points. Always check if your important pieces are safe before making a move somewhere else. Look at what your opponent can do next turn!
outcome.whatToRemember: Before you move, always check: are any of my pieces in danger? Save the valuable ones first!

keyTerms.definition: When your opponent can capture one of your pieces on their next turn. Always look for threats!

outcome-bad.narration: You move the pawn to safety, but your opponent's knight captures your queen! A pawn is only worth 1 point but your queen was worth 9 points. That was a bad trade!

outcome.headline: Oh No! You Saved the Wrong Piece!
outcome.explanation: Saving a pawn instead of your queen is like choosing to save a penny instead of a ten dollar bill! The queen is worth 9 points and the pawn is only worth 1 point. When two pieces are in danger and you can only save one, always save the one that is worth more. Remember the point values: Queen = 9, Rook = 5, Bishop = 3, Knight = 3, Pawn = 1.
outcome.whatToRemember: Always save the more valuable piece! Queen is worth 9, Pawn is worth 1. Save the queen!

keyTerms.definition: When you have more total piece value than your opponent. Losing your queen gives your opponent a big advantage!
